title: ATo from UTG — Too Weak to Open
actionHistory: You are first to act from UTG. No one has acted yet.

options.optionExplanation: Opening ATo from UTG puts you in a tough spot postflop. You'll be out of position against everyone who calls.
Folding is correct. ATo is too weak from UTG because you'll play the entire hand out of position with kicker issues.
Limping from UTG is almost always wrong. It shows weakness and you still have position problems.

explanation: Ace-ten offsuit looks like a decent hand, and from the button it would be a clear open. But from UTG, you have 5-6 players still to act behind you, and if called, you'll be out of position for every postflop street. When you flop top pair with a ten kicker, you'll often be dominated by AJ, AQ, or AK. The positional disadvantage compounds the kicker problem — you can't control the pot effectively out of position. This same hand becomes a raise from the cutoff or button, illustrating the dramatic impact position has on hand playability.
keyConceptSummary: Hands that are profitable from late position can be losing hands from early position. ATo is a prime example.

title: ATo from the Button — Easy Open
actionHistory: Everyone folds to you on the BTN. Only the blinds remain.

options.optionExplanation: Folding ATo on the button when folded to you is far too tight. This is a clear raising hand.
Raising is clearly correct. You have an ace, only the blinds to get through, and you'll have position the entire hand.
Limping wastes your positional advantage. Raising puts pressure on the blinds and builds the pot in position.

explanation: This is the same Ace-Ten offsuit that was a fold from UTG, but now it's a clear raise from the button. What changed? Everything about position. You only have two players to get through (the blinds), and if they call, you'll act last on every street. This information advantage lets you control the pot size, bluff effectively, and extract value when you connect. Position transforms ATo from a marginal hand into a profitable one. This is the single most important lesson in poker: the same cards change value dramatically based on where you sit.
keyConceptSummary: The same hand (ATo) that's a fold from UTG is a clear raise from the button. Position changes everything.
villainRange: SB/BB defending ranges: wide but out of position

title: Button Steal with Weak Holdings
actionHistory: Everyone folds to you on the BTN.

options.optionExplanation: Folding Q7o on the button is too tight when folded to you. You're giving up free profit from blind steals.
Raising to steal the blinds is profitable. You only need the blinds to fold often enough, and position gives you an edge when called.
Limping gives up the initiative and lets the BB see a free flop.

explanation: Q7 offsuit isn't a strong hand, but from the button when folded to you, it's a profitable raise. Here's the math: you're risking 25 to win 15 in blinds. If the blinds fold 63% of the time, you break even — and most players fold more than that. When they do call, you have position for the rest of the hand, allowing you to c-bet the flop and win many pots even when you miss. Button stealing is one of the highest-frequency profit sources in poker. If you're not raising at least 40% of hands on the button when folded to you, you're leaving significant money on the table.
keyConceptSummary: From the button, you can profitably raise with much weaker hands because blind steals succeed often and position protects you when called.
villainRange: SB/BB: will fold most hands, defend with top 30-40%

title: Defending the Big Blind vs Button Open
actionHistory: BTN raises to 25. SB folds. Action on you in the BB.

options.optionExplanation: Folding J8s in the BB vs a button open is too tight. You're getting a great price and have a playable suited hand.
Calling is correct. You already have 10 invested, need to call 15 more into a 40 pot. J8s has enough playability to justify the price.
3-betting J8s is too aggressive against most button opens. You'd be building a big pot out of position with a speculative hand.

explanation: In the big blind, you already have 10 chips invested. When the button raises to 25, you need to call only 15 more to see a flop in a 40-chip pot. That's great pot odds. J8 suited is a perfectly fine defending hand — it can make flushes, straights, and two-pair hands postflop. The key nuance: you ARE out of position, so you need to be selective about which hands you defend with. Suited connectors and suited one-gappers like J8s have good 'playability' — they can flop strong draws and disguised made hands. Offsuit junk like J3o or 92o should still be folded because they flop poorly.
keyConceptSummary: Defend your big blind with suited, connected hands that have postflop playability. The price is usually too good to fold them.
villainRange: BTN opening range: ~40% of hands, very wide

title: Playing Top Pair Out of Position
actionHistory: BTN raised, you called from BB. Flop is K-7-3 rainbow. Action on you.

options.optionExplanation: Checking is correct from out of position. Let the preflop raiser c-bet, then decide whether to call or raise based on their action.
Donk betting with top pair from OOP is suboptimal. It takes the initiative away from the raiser and makes your range easy to read.
A large donk bet bloats the pot out of position, which is exactly what you don't want with a medium-strength hand.

explanation: You have top pair on K-7-3, which is a good hand. But you're out of position, and this changes how you should play it. From OOP, the standard play is to check to the preflop raiser. Why? Because they'll c-bet with most of their range, and you can check-call with your king to keep the pot controlled. If you lead out (donk bet), you take away their chance to bluff and you build a pot that's harder to manage without position. The fundamental principle: out of position with medium-strength hands, keep the pot small and let your opponent define the action. In position, you'd bet this hand for value. Position dictates the line.
keyConceptSummary: Out of position with medium-strength hands, check to the raiser and let them bet. Control the pot size.
villainRange: BTN c-bet range: overpairs, Kx, broadways, air

title: Information Advantage on the Button
actionHistory: You raised BTN, BB called. Flop is A-Q-5 rainbow. BB checks to you.

options.optionExplanation: A small c-bet is perfect. You missed the board but have position. BB's check shows weakness, and this board favors your raising range.
Checking behind gives up the initiative. You have excellent fold equity on a board that favors your range.
A full pot bet risks too much with nothing. A small bet achieves the same fold equity at lower cost.

explanation: You have 9-8 suited on an A-Q-5 board — you've completely missed. But you're on the button, and the BB has checked to you. This is position in action: the BB's check tells you they likely don't have an ace or queen (they would often bet or check-raise with those). This information, which you only have because you act last, makes a small c-bet highly profitable. The board texture also favors your range as the preflop raiser — you're supposed to have more aces and queens than the BB defender. A tiny bet of 1/3 pot will fold out most of BB's range. This play is impossible from out of position because you'd never get this read.
keyConceptSummary: Position gives you information through your opponent's actions. When they show weakness by checking, you can profitably bet with nothing.
villainRange: BB checking range: missed hands, weak pairs, some traps

title: Cutoff Open — Wider Than UTG
actionHistory: Folds to you in the CO. BTN and blinds remain.

options.optionExplanation: Folding KJo from the cutoff is too tight. You only have 3 players behind you and reasonable equity.
Raising is clearly correct. KJo from the CO is a standard open — you'll often steal the blinds, and if called by the blinds you have position.
Limping is almost never correct. Raising puts pressure on the remaining players.

explanation: KJ offsuit is a fold from UTG but a standard open from the cutoff. The difference is that from the CO, you only have three players behind you (BTN, SB, BB), and if the button folds, you'll have position on anyone who calls from the blinds. With fewer players to act through, you can open a wider range profitably. The CO typically opens about 25-30% of hands, compared to UTG's 12-15%. This widening happens because: fewer players means less chance of running into a premium hand, and you'll often end up with the positional advantage postflop.
keyConceptSummary: The cutoff can open significantly wider than early position because fewer players remain behind and you'll often have position postflop.

title: 3-Betting from the Button for Value and Position
actionHistory: CO raises to 25. Action on you on the BTN.

options.optionExplanation: Calling is playable but passive. With AJs in position, you miss the opportunity to build a pot and take control of the hand.
3-betting is optimal. AJs has great equity, you have position, and 3-betting narrows villain's range while building the pot.
Folding AJs on the button is absurdly tight against a CO open.

explanation: AJ suited on the button facing a cutoff open is a textbook 3-bet for value. Here's why position makes this profitable: you'll act last on every street, controlling pot size and information. 3-betting narrows the CO's range — they'll fold hands like KTo, Q9s, and small suited connectors that would have played well postflop. The hands that call your 3-bet (pairs, AK, AQ, KQs) are defined enough for you to play accurately postflop. If you just flat call, the blinds might come along, creating a multiway pot where your hand loses value. Position amplifies the value of 3-betting because you extract maximum postflop advantage.
keyConceptSummary: 3-betting in position builds the pot with an edge and narrows villain's range. Position makes aggressive play more profitable.

title: Small Blind — The Worst Position
actionHistory: BTN raises to 25. You are in the SB. BB still to act.

options.optionExplanation: Folding is correct. T9s is playable from many positions but the SB is the worst seat — you'll be OOP the entire hand with a player behind you.
Calling creates a multiway pot where you're OOP against both the BTN and potentially the BB. T9s doesn't play well enough here.
3-betting T9s from the SB is too aggressive. If called, you're OOP in a big pot with a speculative hand.

explanation: T9 suited is a lovely hand from the button or even the big blind, but from the small blind it's a fold against a button raise. Why? The small blind is the absolute worst position at the table. You act first on every postflop street, which means you never have information before deciding. Additionally, the BB can still squeeze behind you, trapping your call between two opponents. The small blind should play an extremely tight range — mostly 3-bet or fold. Flat calling from the SB is the least profitable play in poker because you guarantee being out of position in a pot where you have no initiative.
keyConceptSummary: The small blind is the worst position. Play tight, prefer 3-bet-or-fold over flat calling. Don't bleed chips from the SB.

title: Controlling the Pot in Position
actionHistory: You raised BTN, BB called. You c-bet flop T-6-2, BB called. Turn K. BB checks.

options.optionExplanation: Checking behind is perfect. You have a medium-strength hand and the king turn is bad for you. Control the pot and see a cheap showdown.
Betting again risks getting check-raised on a turn that improves villain's range. Your top pair is now second pair effectively.
A large bet with a medium-strength hand on a bad turn card is a recipe for losing a big pot.

explanation: This hand beautifully illustrates position's power to control pot size. You have top pair with a nine kicker, which was good on the flop. But the king on the turn changes things — villain's calling range includes Kx hands that just improved to a better pair. From the button, you have the luxury of checking behind to control the pot. This keeps the pot small with a medium-strength hand, gives you a free card to improve, and gets you to a cheap showdown. If you were out of position, you'd face an impossible decision: bet and risk a check-raise, or check and face a bet you can't read. Position gives you this pot-control option that OOP players simply don't have.
keyConceptSummary: Position lets you check behind to control pot size with medium-strength hands. This option doesn't exist out of position.
villainRange: BB calling range after flop call: Tx, Kx, 66, draws, floats
